questions.question: According to Google's official Systems Architecture prep video, what should you NOT expect during this interview round?
options: Writing and debugging production code on a laptop
Discussing how components communicate via APIs
Sketching diagrams on a whiteboard
Estimating throughput and storage requirements
questions.explanation: The video opens by stating there is no coding in the Systems Architecture interview. Candidates may discuss APIs, draw diagrams, and provide quantitative estimates—but not implement code.

questions.question: Google recommends spending approximately how much of the Systems Architecture interview on gathering requirements and presenting an initial solution?
options: About 20 minutes
About 5 minutes
About 45 minutes
The entire hour before any architecture discussion
questions.explanation: The official video notes significant time limits and advises using roughly the first 20 minutes to clarify requirements and present an initial high-level solution before iterating deeper.

questions.question: Why does the Systems Architecture prep video emphasize that time limits are 'significant'?
options: Interviewers want to see how you prioritize, scope an MVP, and communicate under realistic constraints
Candidates who finish early automatically receive a higher level offer
Google does not allow follow-up questions once the timer starts
Time limits exist solely to prevent candidates from using the whiteboard
questions.explanation: The video stresses significant time limits because the round evaluates prioritization, scoping, and communication—not exhaustive perfection. It is not about finishing early or banning clarification.

questions.question: For quantitative estimates and architecture diagrams in the Systems Architecture interview, Google expects you to use:
options: A whiteboard (or paper) rather than relying on an IDE
A personal laptop with your preferred IDE and debugger
Only verbal description with no visual aids permitted
Pre-printed reference architecture posters brought by the candidate
questions.explanation: The SA-V0 segment describes using the whiteboard for estimates and diagrams. The round is not an IDE coding session, and visual communication on the whiteboard is expected—not prohibited or replaced by memorized posters.

questions.question: The official prep video advises candidates to 'think out loud' during the Systems Architecture interview. What does this primarily help interviewers assess?
options: Your reasoning process and how you approach ambiguous problems collaboratively
Your ability to recite memorized system design answers without pause
Your typing speed when implementing services on a laptop
Your knowledge of Google-internal codenames for infrastructure projects
questions.explanation: The SA-V1 segment states that interviewers evaluate communication and process—not just the final diagram. Thinking aloud reveals reasoning, not memorization, typing speed, or internal trivia.

questions.question: Google interviewers in the Systems Architecture round care about your process in addition to your final answer because:
options: Real design work is iterative and collaborative; they need to see how you think, not just what you draw last
The final diagram is never reviewed—only the number of boxes drawn matters
Process is scored only if you use a specific Google-approved template
They have no time to look at diagrams, so only spoken words count
questions.explanation: The video explicitly says process matters, not just the final answer. Interviewers simulate collaborative design work where reasoning and iteration are as important as the endpoint.

questions.question: Systems Architecture problems at Google are deliberately underspecified. What is the intended purpose of this?
options: To test whether you ask clarifying questions and define scope before committing to architecture
To trick candidates into choosing the wrong database so interviewers can end the loop early
To ensure every candidate produces an identical canonical diagram
To avoid any discussion of scale or reliability requirements
questions.explanation: The video states problems are deliberately underspecified so interviewers can observe whether candidates clarify requirements—a core communication skill—not to trap them or force identical answers.

questions.question: When faced with an underspecified design prompt, what does Google's prep video recommend as a strong first move?
options: Ask clarifying questions about scale, constraints, and use cases before drawing architecture
Immediately present a detailed sharding scheme without confirming requirements
Wait silently until the interviewer fills in every detail without prompting
Decline to answer until given a complete 100-page specification document
questions.explanation: The SA-V1 segment highlights asking clarifying questions as essential. Jumping to architecture, staying silent, or refusing to engage miss the collaborative clarification phase the interview is designed to test.

questions.question: The official video notes there are often multiple valid solutions to a Systems Architecture problem. What does this imply for candidates?
options: You should explain trade-offs among reasonable approaches rather than hunting for one secretly 'correct' design
You will be penalized unless your diagram matches the interviewer's preferred solution exactly
You must enumerate every possible architecture before choosing one at random
Only designs used in production at Google are considered valid
questions.explanation: Multiple valid solutions means interviewers evaluate trade-off reasoning and coherence—not whether you guessed a single right answer or copied an internal Google system.

questions.question: The Systems Architecture interview is described as collaborative problem-solving. Which behavior best reflects this?
options: Engaging the interviewer in a dialogue—confirming assumptions, responding to hints, and refining the design together
Treating the session as a solo performance with no interaction until the final 5 minutes
Asking the interviewer to write the entire design while you observe silently
Insisting your first answer is perfect and refusing to incorporate feedback
questions.explanation: Collaborative problem-solving means dialogue throughout—clarifying, iterating, and responding to the interviewer—not monologue, delegation, or rigid adherence to a first draft.

questions.question: When Google asks you to design a system for 'planet-scale' or 'global users,' they are primarily testing whether you can:
options: Reason about worldwide distribution, capacity, and cross-region constraints—not just a single-datacenter deployment
Optimize a single-server deployment for minimum memory usage only
Design exclusively for localhost development environments
Ignore latency because the internet is uniformly fast everywhere
questions.explanation: The SA-V2 segment introduces planet-scale and global users as core evaluation themes—requiring geographic distribution, capacity planning, and latency awareness beyond single-machine thinking.

questions.question: At scale, Google expects your design to address both reliability and efficient use of resources. Which approach best satisfies both goals?
options: Eliminate single points of failure while right-sizing components and avoiding over-provisioning without redundancy
Maximize redundancy by replicating every byte of data on every machine with no regard for cost
Minimize cost by running everything on a single server with no backups
Prioritize resource efficiency by disabling all monitoring and health checks
questions.explanation: The video pairs reliability with efficient resource use—redundancy and fault tolerance without wasteful over-engineering. Extreme redundancy without cost awareness or eliminating redundancy/monitoring fail both goals.

questions.question: The prep video states you should be able to explain how you would know if your system is working. This most directly relates to:
options: Monitoring, health checks, metrics, and alerting to detect degradation or failures
Assuming the system works unless a user sends a complaint email
Checking only at deployment time and never again during operation
Relying on interviewers to monitor your design after the session ends
questions.explanation: Knowing whether a system works requires observability—metrics, health checks, and alerting. The video treats operability at scale as part of the design exercise, not post-hoc user complaints.

questions.question: Identifying bottlenecks is a key skill tested in the Systems Architecture interview. Which question best demonstrates bottleneck analysis?
options: At 1M requests/sec, will the database connection pool, network bandwidth, or single hot partition limit throughput first?
Which color should the load balancer icon be on the whiteboard?
What is the interviewer's favorite sorting algorithm for linked lists?
How many whiteboard markers are available in the room?
questions.explanation: The SA-V2 segment calls out identifying bottlenecks—connecting load to limiting components like connection pools, network, or hot partitions. Cosmetic, trivia, or logistical questions do not demonstrate this skill.

questions.question: Google expects candidates to define APIs between components in a scaled design. Why are explicit APIs important?
options: They establish clear contracts for data flow, enable independent scaling, and make failure domains explicit
They replace the need for any discussion of storage or replication
They are required only for REST but never for internal RPC or message queues
They exist solely to increase the number of boxes on the whiteboard
questions.explanation: The video mentions APIs between components as a scaling primitive—defining contracts and boundaries. APIs complement (not replace) storage design and apply beyond any single protocol style.

questions.question: When a dataset is too large for a single machine, the official video suggests sharding it across worker machines. What problem does sharding primarily solve?
options: Storage and compute capacity limits by partitioning data so each worker handles a subset
Syntax errors in the candidate's pseudocode
The need to eliminate all network communication between machines
Compliance with a requirement to use exactly one physical server
questions.explanation: Sharding/partitioning distributes a large dataset across workers to overcome single-machine storage and throughput limits. It does not eliminate networking or mandate a single server.

questions.question: The video mentions patterns like a pool of machines where the fastest worker handles the next task. This pattern primarily improves:
options: Throughput and latency for parallelizable work by utilizing multiple workers dynamically
Strong consistency by forcing all writes through a single global lock forever
Data durability by storing everything only in browser localStorage
Security by removing the need for authentication entirely
questions.explanation: Worker pools and fastest-worker patterns distribute parallelizable tasks across machines to improve throughput and reduce tail latency. They are not about global locks, client-only storage, or skipping auth.

questions.question: When discussing system properties at scale, latency refers to:
options: The time delay experienced for an individual operation or request to complete
The total number of machines in the data center regardless of response time
The annual cost of cloud storage contracts
The number of engineers assigned to the project
questions.explanation: The SA-V2 segment lists latency alongside throughput and storage as key properties. Latency is per-operation delay—not machine count, cost, or team size.

questions.question: Throughput in a scaled system design context measures:
options: How many operations or requests the system can handle per unit of time
The physical weight of server racks in the data center
The number of whiteboard diagrams drawn per interview
The chronological age of the oldest database replica
questions.explanation: Throughput is the rate of work processed (e.g., requests per second)—one of the three properties (latency, throughput, storage) the video asks candidates to reason about quantitatively.

questions.question: When Google asks about storage properties of your design, they expect you to address:
options: How much data accumulates over time, retention policies, and whether storage growth is sustainable at scale
Only the in-memory cache size with no consideration of persistent data
The storage requirements of the interviewer's laptop exclusively
Storage only if the product is a file system—never for databases or logs
questions.explanation: Storage properties include growth rate, retention, and capacity planning at scale. Nearly every scaled system (logs, databases, media) has storage implications—not just file systems or caches alone.

questions.question: The prep video emphasizes providing numeric estimates (e.g., requests per second) with justification. Why is justification important?
options: It shows you can connect assumptions to reality and sanity-check whether the design is feasible
It allows you to skip discussing trade-offs entirely
It proves you memorized exact Google production traffic numbers
It replaces the need to discuss failure modes or replication
questions.explanation: The SA-V3 segment wants concrete, justified estimates grounded in assumptions—not memorized internal stats. Estimation supports feasibility analysis and complements (not replaces) trade-offs and failure discussion.

questions.question: Google expects Systems Architecture answers to respect the 'laws of physics' or physical reality. Which claim violates this principle?
options: A design that serves 1 billion users globally with 1 ms read latency from a single disk in one datacenter for all users
A design that uses regional caches to reduce average read latency for geographically distributed users
A design that acknowledges cross-continent RTT as a lower bound on synchronous global operations
A design that shards data to parallelize I/O across many machines
questions.explanation: Physical reality includes speed-of-light latency limits and I/O bounds. Serving global users with 1 ms reads from one disk ignores geography and hardware limits; the other options acknowledge real constraints.

questions.question: According to the official video, which ordering correctly ranks typical access latency from FASTEST to SLOWEST?
options: Memory read → Disk read → LAN round-trip → Cross-continental round-trip
Cross-continental round-trip → Memory read → Disk read → LAN round-trip
Disk read → Memory read → Cross-continental round-trip → LAN round-trip
LAN round-trip → Cross-continental round-trip → Memory read → Disk read
questions.explanation: The SA-V3 segment presents a relative cost/latency hierarchy: memory reads are fastest, then disk, then LAN RTT, then cross-continental RTT. This underpins napkin-math and caching decisions.

questions.question: Why does the prep video distinguish LAN round-trip time from cross-continental round-trip time?
options: Geographic distance and network hops make cross-continent RTT orders of magnitude larger, affecting synchronous global designs
They are identical in latency so the distinction is purely cosmetic
LAN RTT is always slower than disk reads for every workload
Cross-continent RTT applies only to wireless Bluetooth connections
questions.explanation: The video explicitly contrasts LAN vs cross-continental RTT because global synchronous operations hit speed-of-light and routing delays—often dominating latency budgets compared to local network hops.

questions.question: Sharding is listed in the video as an industry pattern Google expects you to know. In an interview, demonstrating sharding knowledge means you can explain:
options: How to partition data by a key so each shard holds a disjoint subset and scales independently
How to store all data in a single process heap without any boundaries
How to encrypt passwords using bcrypt exclusively
How to reverse a linked list in O(n) time
questions.explanation: Sharding is a partitioning strategy for scale—disjoint subsets by key. Password hashing and linked-list algorithms are coding interview topics, not the sharding pattern the Systems Architecture video references.

questions.question: The video mentions replication types as patterns you should understand. Why might you choose asynchronous replication over synchronous replication?
options: To reduce write latency and tolerate temporary lag between replicas, accepting possible stale reads
To guarantee zero data loss in all failure scenarios with no latency penalty
To eliminate the need for any copies of data entirely
To ensure every read always reflects the latest write globally with no coordination cost
questions.explanation: Async replication trades stronger immediate consistency for lower write latency and higher availability under partition—interviewers expect you to articulate this trade-off. The other options misstate replication properties.

questions.question: Write-ahead logging (WAL) is cited as a pattern Google expects you to know. What problem does WAL primarily address?
options: Durability: ensuring committed writes survive process crashes by persisting changes to log before applying them
UI responsiveness: rendering web pages faster in the browser
Load balancing: distributing HTTP requests across servers
Compression: reducing image file sizes in a CDN
questions.explanation: WAL ensures durability by logging changes before applying them to storage so crashes can recover committed data. It is a storage durability pattern—not UI, LB, or CDN optimization.

questions.question: Separating data from metadata and distributing load are patterns mentioned in the video. What is a typical benefit of separating metadata from bulk data?
options: Metadata (indexes, routing tables) can be replicated and queried quickly while bulk data is stored cheaply at larger scale
It eliminates all need for replication or caching anywhere in the system
It forces all users to download the entire dataset on every request
It prevents you from sharding because metadata and data must always live on one machine
questions.explanation: Separating metadata from bulk data allows fast routing/index lookups on small, replicated metadata while storing large payloads efficiently—a common large-system pattern the video highlights alongside load distribution.

questions.question: The SA-V4 segment asks candidates to identify systematic shortcomings in their designs. Which response best demonstrates this skill?
options: Acknowledging that a single-region design cannot survive regional outages and proposing multi-region failover
Claiming the design has no weaknesses because any critique means the interviewer is wrong
Refusing to discuss weaknesses until after receiving an offer
Listing only cosmetic UI issues while ignoring data loss and availability risks
questions.explanation: Identifying systematic shortcomings means honestly analyzing failure modes and limits—e.g., single-region vulnerability—not denying flaws or focusing on trivia.

questions.question: When interviewers ask how your system responds to failures, they expect you to describe:
options: Detection (monitoring/alerts), mitigation (retries, failover, degradation), and recovery procedures
Only happy-path behavior under zero load conditions
How to blame individual engineers in postmortems as a first step
Failure response is out of scope for Systems Architecture interviews
questions.explanation: The video explicitly covers failure response—detecting problems and designing mitigation/recovery. Happy-path-only answers or claiming failure is out of scope miss a core evaluation criterion.

questions.question: The video gives rotating disk vs flash as an example trade-off. Choosing rotating disk over flash primarily trades:
options: Lower storage cost for higher latency and slower random I/O
Higher storage cost for lower latency in all access patterns
Better durability for worse durability
Elimination of all replication needs for mandatory synchronous global writes
questions.explanation: The SA-V4 example: disk offers cheaper storage at the cost of higher latency compared to flash/SSD. The other options invert or confuse the trade-off.

questions.question: Google interviewers want trade-offs laid out explicitly. Which statement BEST demonstrates explicit trade-off reasoning?
options: We choose async replication because it lowers write latency, accepting that readers may briefly see stale data
We choose async replication because it is always superior to every alternative in every scenario
We choose async replication because the interviewer mentioned the word 'database'
We choose async replication with no explanation because trade-offs are irrelevant
questions.explanation: Explicit trade-offs name both the benefit and the cost. Claiming universal superiority, random choice, or ignoring trade-offs fails the SA-V4 communication standard.

questions.question: The video advises considering multiple solutions, then committing to one and iterating. What is the purpose of the 'commit' step?
options: To drive the conversation forward with a concrete design the interviewer can probe, rather than endlessly listing options
To lock in the first idea permanently with no changes allowed afterward
To avoid any discussion of alternatives because they waste time
To copy the interviewer's solution verbatim without understanding it
questions.explanation: Considering alternatives then committing means making a reasoned choice to deepen the design—not rigid permanence, avoiding alternatives entirely, or uncritical copying.

questions.question: After committing to an initial design, the SA-V4 segment expects you to iterate. A strong iteration might include:
options: Adding replication or caching after the interviewer introduces a new failure or scale constraint
Erasing the whiteboard and restarting with an unrelated coding problem
Refusing to change anything because iteration shows weakness
Switching topics to leadership behavioral questions mid-design
questions.explanation: Iteration means refining the design when probed with new constraints—adding redundancy, caching, etc. Refusing to adapt or changing interview rounds misses the iterative design process.

questions.question: The best-practices segment emphasizes explaining your thought process. This is important because interviewers score:
options: Your problem-solving approach and communication, not only raw technical vocabulary
Only whether you use the exact same diagram as previous candidates
Whether you finish silently without asking any questions
Your ability to compile code during the session
questions.explanation: SA-V5 states that problem-solving approach is scored alongside technical ability. Silent completion, identical diagrams, or coding are not the focus of this round.

questions.question: According to the video, your problem-solving approach may be scored separately from your technical ability. Which behavior aligns with a strong problem-solving approach?
options: Breaking the problem into requirements, estimates, components, trade-offs, and failure modes in a structured sequence
Jumping directly to a specific database brand without clarifying access patterns
Avoiding all numbers because estimation is discouraged
Waiting for the interviewer to design the entire system while you take notes passively
questions.explanation: Structured decomposition—requirements, estimates, components, trade-offs, failures—demonstrates problem-solving approach. Random tech choices, avoiding math, or passive note-taking do not.

questions.question: Systems Architecture questions are open-ended by design. What should you do when a prompt feels vague?
options: Ask for clarification on scope, scale, and constraints rather than guessing silently
Assume the most complex possible interpretation and refuse to simplify
Answer with a single word and wait for the interview to end
Tell the interviewer the question is too open-ended and stop participating
questions.explanation: Open-ended prompts invite clarification—a best practice explicitly repeated in SA-V5. Extreme assumptions, minimal answers, or disengaging fail the collaborative intent.

questions.question: After presenting a first solution, a strong candidate should be prepared to:
options: Improve on the initial answer when the interviewer adds constraints or points out gaps
Insist the first answer is final and cannot be changed
Apologize and end the interview if any flaw is identified
Ignore feedback because only the first 5 minutes of the interview count
questions.explanation: SA-V5 explicitly recommends improving on your first answer—iteration under feedback is a positive signal, not a failure. Rigidity, quitting, or ignoring probes work against you.

questions.question: Google recommends practicing Systems Architecture problems on paper or a whiteboard without an IDE. This practice helps you:
options: Build fluency in diagramming, estimation, and verbal explanation—the skills actually tested in the interview
Memorize syntax for every programming language Google uses
Avoid learning any distributed systems concepts because tools replace knowledge
Prepare exclusively for coding interviews instead of architecture rounds
questions.explanation: Whiteboard practice builds the communication, diagramming, and estimation skills the round evaluates. Syntax memorization or skipping systems knowledge misses the point; this round is not a coding interview.

questions.question: Which prep habit from the SA-V5 best-practices segment most reduces the risk of building the wrong architecture?
options: Asking clarifying questions about requirements before committing to component boundaries
Selecting technologies alphabetically from a list to appear unbiased
Drawing as many boxes as possible without labels to show complexity
Skipping estimation because numbers are never discussed in this interview
questions.explanation: Asking clarifying questions before committing prevents wrong-architecture drift—a capstone best practice in SA-V5 alongside whiteboard practice and iterative improvement. Unlabeled complexity and skipping math are anti-patterns the video warns against.
